title: Adjoint-based Perfusion Estimation from Dynamic Contrast-Enhanced Ultrasound: Advection-Diffusion and Two-Compartment Models
summary: Tumor perfusion and vascular properties are important determinants of a cancer's response to therapy. In this paper, we discuss the estimation of spatially varying blood flow velocities and perfusion parameters from time-resolved contrast agent concentration data. We compare a standard parabolic advection-diffusion model against a two-compartment model governed by a coupled system of hyperbolic advection-reaction equations, which is physiologically more sound. To address the inherent ill-posedness of this parameter identification problem, we employ Tikhonov regularization and derive continuous adjoint equations necessary for efficient, gradient-based minimization. We discuss the numerical discretization of the state and adjoint systems using state-of-the-art schemes, and demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed reconstruction algorithms through numerical experiments on synthetic data and in vivo dynamic contrast-enhanced ultrasound measurements.

processed.summary:
- Proposes an **adjoint-based framework** for estimating spatially varying blood flow velocities and tumor perfusion parameters from ultrasound data.
- Compares a **two-compartment hyperbolic model** against a standard advection-diffusion model, proving the former is more physiologically sound for cancer therapy response.
- Utilizes **Tikhonov regularization** and continuous adjoint equations to solve the ill-posed parameter identification problem efficiently.
- Demonstrates high-accuracy **numerical reconstruction** on both synthetic datasets and in vivo dynamic contrast-enhanced ultrasound measurements.
key_results: Derivation of continuous adjoint equations for complex hyperbolic advection-reaction systems.
Identification of spatially varying flow velocities from time-resolved contrast agent data.
Validation that two-compartment models better capture physiological dynamics than standard parabolic models.
Successful application of gradient-based minimization for high-dimensional parameter estimation.
Demonstration of efficacy using real-world in vivo dynamic contrast-enhanced ultrasound (DCE-US) measurements.

title: Shield-Loco: Shielding Locomotion Policies with Predictive Safety Filtering
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) policies enable dynamic legged locomotion but lack mechanisms to avoid violations of safety constraints that are absent during training. Large-scale offline safe learning is impractical for covering all edge cases. Existing safety frameworks either rely on reduced-order models that cannot reason about whole-body behaviors or require conservative recovery controllers that degrade task performance. We propose a predictive safety filter that post-hoc filters the nominal contact locations fed to the RL policy. When a collision is predicted, a sampling-based optimizer asynchronously searches for safer contact sequences using a full-physics model, while a learned value function bootstraps long-horizon returns. Our three algorithmic components (geometric projection of sampled contacts, momentum-augmented updates, and replica-exchange) make the optimization tractable in a discontinuous contact landscape. We validate the filter on a quadruped robot in dense, cluttered environments, both in simulation and in the real world, showing substantial reductions in safety violations with minimal deviation from the nominal input.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **Shield-Loco**, a post-hoc safety filter that modifies nominal contact locations for RL-based quadruped locomotion policies to avoid collisions.
- Employs a **sampling-based optimizer** that utilizes a full-physics model and a learned value function to asynchronously search for safe contact sequences.
- Implements novel algorithmic components including **geometric projection**, momentum-augmented updates, and **replica-exchange** to handle discontinuous contact landscapes.
- Demonstrates a robust mechanism for shielding existing RL policies from edge cases in cluttered environments without requiring extensive retraining.

Maintains high task performance with minimal deviation from the nominal input contact locations.
Optimization remains tractable in real-time through the use of a learned value function for long-horizon returns.
Successfully handles complex whole-body behaviors that reduced-order models typically fail to capture.
Validated via real-world hardware deployment on a quadruped robot, proving sim-to-real transferability.

title: OPTIMUS-Prime: Minimal and Sufficient Concept Explanations for Deep Vision Models
summary: The growing demand for transparency in automated decision-making has propelled eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI) to the forefront of machine learning research. In computer vision, however, existing explanation methods often prioritize end-user accessibility at the expense of formal guarantees, leaving a critical gap between practical utility and theoretical rigor. In this paper, we address this gap by introducing OPTIMUS, a novel framework for generating concept-based visual explanations for deep classification models. OPTIMUS explanations take the form of visual heatmaps that not only remain interpretable to end users, but are grounded in the well-established theory of prime implicants, providing formal guarantees that have been largely absent from existing saliency-based methods. Specifically, OPTIMUS explanations satisfy two desirable properties: sufficiency, ensuring that the highlighted concepts provably guarantee the classifier's prediction, and minimality, ensuring that no strict subset of those concepts retains this guarantee. Together, these properties yield explanations that are both logically tight and visually coherent. We validate our approach on a visual classification benchmark, demonstrating that OPTIMUS heatmaps naturally and faithfully surface the decision-relevant concepts underlying model predictions.

processed.summary:
• The OPTIMUS framework introduces a novel approach to **eXplainable Artificial Intelligence (XAI)** by generating concept-based visual explanations for deep classification models.
• Unlike traditional saliency methods, it is grounded in the theory of **prime implicants**, providing formal mathematical guarantees for explanation quality.
• The architecture prioritizes **sufficiency**, ensuring highlighted concepts prove the classifier's prediction, and **minimality**, ensuring no redundant features are included.
• The system outputs **visual heatmaps** that bridge the gap between human interpretability and theoretical rigor for automated decision-making.
key_results: Development of the OPTIMUS framework for logically grounded visual explanations.
Mathematical integration of prime implicant theory into deep vision model transparency.
Formal proof that generated explanations are sufficient to guarantee specific model predictions.
Achievement of 'minimality' in concept sets, preventing visual noise in heatmaps.
Empirical validation on classification benchmarks demonstrating high faithfulness to model logic.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Imperial College London researchers introduce OPTIMUS, providing formally guaranteed, minimal, and sufficient concept explanations for deep vision models.

title: Seeing Without Exposing: Adaptive Privacy Control for Open-World, Context-Hungry MLLMs
summary: Multimodal large language models (MLLMs) have raised new privacy challenges. On the data side, user-provided inputs often include unpredictable sensitive information; while on the downstream task side, model reasoning depends on rich visual context that may itself be privacy-sensitive. Existing privacy protection methods, however, rely on predefined sensitive categories and fixed obfuscation strategies, struggling to tackle such challenges in MLLMs. To address this dilemma, we propose Anchored Privacy Drifting (APD), a training-free method that drifts privacy-sensitive elements toward semantically equivalent alternatives while anchoring contextual cues to the source image. To systematically evaluate this dual objective of privacy protection and contextual preservation, we introduce AdaptShield, a comprehensive benchmark covering 22 privacy categories, which combines conventional privacy metrics with MLLM-based assessments of contextual utility. Extensive experiments show that our method achieves balanced improvements in both privacy sanitization and content retention, with average gains of 10.4% on textual categories and 8.5% under MLLM-based evaluation across four MLLM series, i.e., Qwen2.5, Qwen3, InternVL3, and InternVL3.5.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Anchored Privacy Drifting (APD)**, a training-free framework designed to sanitize visual inputs for MLLMs by replacing sensitive elements with semantically equivalent alternatives.
- Utilizes a dual-objective mechanism that **anchors contextual cues** to the source image while **drifting privacy-sensitive features**, ensuring the model retains reasoning capabilities without data exposure.
- Establishes the **AdaptShield** benchmark, a comprehensive suite covering 22 privacy categories that integrates traditional metrics with MLLM-based assessments of contextual utility.
key_results: Achieved an average improvement of 10.4% in privacy protection across textual categories.
Demonstrated an 8.5% increase in content retention based on MLLM-driven contextual evaluations.
Successfully validated on state-of-the-art models including Qwen2.5, Qwen3, InternVL3, and InternVL3.5.
Provides a training-free solution to the 'context-hungry' nature of MLLMs without requiring model fine-tuning.
Identifies and addresses 22 distinct privacy categories, moving beyond fixed obfuscation strategies.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Anchored Privacy Drifting to sanitize MLLM visual inputs while preserving reasoning context via semantic drifting.

title: Textual Supervision Enhances Geospatial Representations in Vision-Language Models
summary: Geospatial understanding is a critical yet underexplored dimension in the development of machine learning systems for tasks such as image geolocation and spatial reasoning. In this work, we analyze the geospatial representations acquired by three model families: vision-only architectures (e.g., ViT), vision-language models (e.g., CLIP), and large-scale multimodal foundation models (e.g., LLaVA, Qwen, and Gemma). By evaluating across image clusters, including people, landmarks, and everyday objects, grouped based on the degree of localizability, we reveal systematic gaps in spatial accuracy and show that textual supervision enhances the learning of geospatial representations. Our findings suggest the role of language as an effective complementary modality for encoding spatial context and multimodal learning as a key direction for advancing geospatial AI.

processed.summary:
* Systematic evaluation of **geospatial representations** across three model families: vision-only (**ViT**), vision-language (**CLIP**), and large-scale multimodal foundation models (**LLaVA**, **Qwen**, **Gemma**).
* Investigates how **textual supervision** acts as a semantic anchor to bridge the gap in spatial accuracy within visual architectures.
* Analyzes performance across varying degrees of **localizability**, identifying systematic failures in vision-only models when processing everyday objects versus landmarks.
* Proposes that **multimodal learning** is the essential architectural direction for advancing **Geospatial AI** and image geolocation tasks.
key_results: Textual supervision significantly enhances the learning of geospatial representations compared to vision-only training.
Vision-language models like CLIP show superior spatial grounding over standard ViT architectures.
Landmark-based clusters demonstrate higher localization accuracy than clusters containing people or everyday objects.
Large-scale multimodal foundation models (LLaVA, Qwen) exhibit the most robust spatial reasoning capabilities.
Language serves as an effective complementary modality for encoding complex geographic and spatial contexts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that integrating textual supervision improves geospatial accuracy in multimodal models compared to vision-only architectures.

title: When Recovery Matters: The Blind Spot of Surrogate Privacy in MLLM Editing
summary: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) enable flexible instruction-driven image editing, but privacy risks arise when user images expose diverse and user-specific private content. Canonical privacy protection strategies typically substitute sensitive regions with surrogate content before cloud editing. Yet, the resulting output is often an edited surrogate rather than the desired edited source image, neglecting the local recovery in both design and evaluation scope. To this end, we introduce SPPE (Surrogate-based Privacy-Preserving Editing), the first recovery-oriented benchmark covering 36 fine-grained privacy categories and 65 editing instructions. It defines two complementary tasks: 1) editability assessment, which estimates before cloud interaction whether a surrogate can induce an edit consistent with the original image; and 2) surrogate-to-source edit recovery, which evaluates whether the edited surrogate can be transferred back to the private source with the edit effect preserved. We address each task with a dedicated method: ERMA predicts surrogate editability through instruction-aware multimodal relation modeling, while \method performs cycle-consistent recovery by using the surrogate editing pair as visual edit evidence and the source image as a source-preserving anchor. Experiments on SPPE and InstructPix2Pix show consistent improvements on both tasks. For editability assessment, ERMA improves over the best-performing baselines by 13.9% in SRCC and 12.3% in PLCC. For surrogate-to-source edit recovery, C2E-S2SER outperforms SOER across all 8 source integrity and edit consistency metrics on SPPE.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **SPPE (Surrogate-based Privacy-Preserving Editing)**, the first benchmark dedicated to recovering private source images after surrogate-based cloud editing.
• Proposes **ERMA**, an instruction-aware multimodal relation model that predicts the editability of surrogates before cloud interaction.
• Develops **C2E-S2SER**, a cycle-consistent recovery framework that utilizes surrogate pairs as visual evidence to transfer edits back to source images while maintaining integrity.
• Addresses the "blind spot" in current privacy workflows where sensitive region substitution leads to unusable surrogate-only outputs.
key_results: Established a comprehensive benchmark with 36 fine-grained privacy categories and 65 editing instructions.
ERMA achieved a 13.9% improvement in SRCC and 12.3% in PLCC for editability assessment tasks.
C2E-S2SER outperformed the previous SOER baseline across all 8 source integrity and edit consistency metrics.
Demonstrated that instruction-aware modeling is superior to vanilla multimodal similarity for surrogate evaluation.
Validated the cycle-consistent approach on InstructPix2Pix, showing robust recovery of source-preserving anchors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce SPPE to bridge the critical gap between privacy-preserving surrogate editing and original source image recovery.

title: UrduMMLU: A Massive Multitask Benchmark for Urdu Language Understanding
summary: Meaningful multilingual evaluation must test models in the target language and educational context. Urdu, spoken by more than 230 million people, lacks a broad MMLU-style benchmark built from native educational sources. We introduce UrduMMLU, a benchmark of 26,431 Urdu MCQs across 26 subjects and five domains, collected from native Urdu MCQ banks and public examination PDFs. Unlike translation-based resources, UrduMMLU covers both standard academic subjects and Urdu- and region-specific content. We label the exam-derived portion through dual human annotation with strict consensus filtering. We evaluate 30 LLMs under English and Urdu prompts, yielding 60 zero-shot evaluations, and further evaluate four open-source LLMs under multiple few-shot settings across both prompt languages. Gemini-3.5-Flash performs best, reaching 90.20% and 90.34% accuracy, while no other model exceeds 85%. The strongest open-source model trails by 7.79 and 8.92 points, and many models lose 25 to 40 points on Urdu-centered Humanities subjects compared with STEM. Few-shot prompting yields only modest gains. UrduMMLU shows that Urdu knowledge remains uneven in current LLMs, especially for regionally grounded content.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **UrduMMLU**, a comprehensive benchmark of 26,431 native Urdu MCQs across 26 subjects designed to evaluate **Multilingual LLM** performance.
• Utilizes **human-annotated native sources** rather than machine translations to capture regional nuances and specific educational contexts in Urdu.
• Benchmarks 30 LLMs using **zero-shot and few-shot** prompting, providing a rigorous framework for evaluating model performance in low-resource linguistic environments.
• Highlights a significant **architectural gap** between proprietary and open-source models in handling regionally grounded knowledge versus standard STEM subjects.
key_results: Gemini-3.5-Flash achieved the highest accuracy at 90.34%, the only model to exceed 85%.
Open-source models trail proprietary leaders by approximately 7.8 to 8.9 percentage points.
Models exhibit a performance drop of 25 to 40 points in Urdu Humanities compared to STEM subjects.
Few-shot prompting provides only marginal gains over zero-shot settings for most models.
The benchmark identifies a persistent lack of regional alignment in current large language models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce UrduMMLU, a native-source benchmark revealing that proprietary models significantly outperform open-source alternatives in regional knowledge.

title: Implicit Structural Modeling via Generative Diffusion Frameworks
summary: Implicit structural modeling can support understanding subsurface spatial configurations, revealing patterns of geological evolution, and enabling quantitative simulation of geological processes, thereby offering substantial scientific and engineering value. Conventional approaches formulate it as an optimization problem or framework interpolation to fit a continuous scalar field, whereas machine learning methods typically adopt discriminative regression to directly predict implicit models. However, in complex scenarios involving fault intersections, branching, and thrust nappes, these methods still struggle to maintain topological consistency and kinematic plausibility. In this work, we develop an implicit structural modeling approach based on diffusion models. We construct a set of training data through a simulation based synthesis pipeline and design a dedicated encoder for conditional injection, allowing the conditional branch to converge rapidly while effectively reinforcing the input conditional priors throughout the diffusion process, thereby more stably propagating structural constraints. We then inject these conditional features into a backbone network pretrained on large scale natural images to enable conditional training of the diffusion model. Although our synthetic data include only a relatively stylized normal fault system, experiments demonstrate strong generalization, enabling the model to effectively handle diverse complex structural types such as strike slip faults and intricate flower fault systems. More importantly, even in challenging thrust nappe settings where the scalar field becomes non monotonic and exhibits abrupt depth discontinuities, the model can still generate reliable implicit structural models.

processed.summary:
- The paper introduces a **generative diffusion framework** for implicit structural modeling, shifting the paradigm from traditional optimization or discriminative regression to a probabilistic generative approach.
- Utilizes a **dedicated conditional encoder** and injection branch that allows the model to converge rapidly while reinforcing structural priors throughout the diffusion process.
- Employs a **pre-trained backbone** on large-scale natural images, demonstrating that transfer learning from general visual domains can enhance specialized geological modeling tasks.
- Addresses critical failures in existing methods, specifically maintaining **topological consistency** and kinematic plausibility in complex fault systems and non-monotonic scalar fields.
key_results: Achieved high topological consistency in complex fault intersections where regression methods typically fail.
Successfully generalized to strike-slip and flower fault systems despite being trained on simpler stylized normal faults.
Maintained model reliability in challenging thrust nappe settings with abrupt depth discontinuities.
Developed a simulation-based synthesis pipeline that effectively generates training data for subsurface modeling.
Demonstrated that the conditional injection mechanism effectively propagates structural constraints throughout the entire diffusion process.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce a diffusion-based framework that generates topologically consistent subsurface structural models, outperforming traditional regression methods.

title: TraRA: Trajectory-level Recognition Aggregation for Video Text Spotting in Urban Surveillance
summary: Video Text Spotting (VTS) is essential for urban surveillance and intelligent transportation systems, enabling automated reading of street signs, vehicle markings, and scene text in video streams. However, reliable recognition remains challenging due to dynamic video factors common in surveillance scenarios, including motion blur, occlusion, and scale variation, which degrade frame-level recognition. Existing VTS methods typically perform recognition independently on each frame, leading to inconsistent and inaccurate results across sequences. To address these limitations, we propose TraRA (Trajectory-level Recognition Aggregation for VTS), a plug-and-play method that performs trajectory-level text recognition by leveraging temporal and multimodal consistency. TraRA integrates two key modules: (1) the Temporal Clustering and (2) the Vision-Language Aggregation. The former refines noisy trajectories by grouping temporally and visually coherent text instances, while the latter employs a Low-Rank Adaptation-enhanced Vision-Language model to fuse visual cues with linguistic context across frames. By aggregating information over entire text trajectories, TraRA achieves robust text recognition even under challenging surveillance conditions. Extensive experiments on four public benchmarks, including road and urban scene datasets (RoadText, BOVText, ArTVideo, and ICDAR15), demonstrate that TraRA consistently improves tracking and recognition performance over state-of-the-art VTS methods. The source code is available at https://github.com/trid2912/TraRA.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **TraRA**, a plug-and-play framework for **Video Text Spotting (VTS)** that shifts from inconsistent frame-level recognition to robust **trajectory-level aggregation**.
- Utilizes a **Temporal Clustering** module to refine noisy text trajectories by grouping visually and temporally coherent instances across video sequences.
- Implements a **Vision-Language Aggregation** module leveraging **Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA)** to fuse visual cues with linguistic context via multimodal foundation models.
- Effectively counters common surveillance challenges like **motion blur, occlusion, and scale variation** by synthesizing information across an entire object's temporal span.
key_results: Achieved consistent performance gains over state-of-the-art VTS methods on RoadText, BOVText, ArTVideo, and ICDAR15.
Successfully mitigated frame-level recognition errors caused by dynamic video factors in urban environments.
Validated the plug-and-play compatibility of the TraRA module with existing video text tracking pipelines.
Demonstrated that LoRA-enhanced VLMs significantly improve the fusion of visual and linguistic context for text recognition.
Established a new benchmark for trajectory-level recognition stability in intelligent transportation system scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce TraRA, using LoRA-enhanced vision-language models to aggregate video text trajectories for superior surveillance accuracy.

title: Synthetic APTs: the Collapse of TTP-Based Attribution
summary: Cyber Threat Intelligence CTI attribution relies on identifying the Tactics, Techniques, and Procedures TTPs that distinguish one threat actor from another. This approach presupposes that each adversary leaves a recognizable operational fingerprint. This work investigates whether AI driven adversary emulation challenges that presupposition. We deploy agents from our Cybersecurity SuperIntelligence CSI framework, configured as five Advanced Persistent Threat APT groups, APT28, APT29, APT41, APT44, and Lazarus Group, against AI driven Defender agents across two cyber ranges provided by CYBER RANGES, equipped with defensive software Wazuh, Velociraptor, Elasticsearch and active AI driven defenders: an enterprise network and a military infrastructure. Across 20 experiments using two defender models, a binary pattern emerges: all 10 Enterprise range experiments resulted in compromise 2 to 12 hosts per experiment, while all 10 Military range experiments were successfully defended or resulted in stalemates, regardless of APT profile or defender model. In 8 of 10 Enterprise experiments, attackers independently weaponized the defender's own Velociraptor endpoint management platform as a command and control channel, a convergent behavior not encoded in any threat intelligence profile. We argue that in the AI era, wherein agents can be deployed provided the right models are available and subject to the right scaffolding and agentic configuration, the entry barrier for operating like a nation state APT collapses: beyond nation states, individuals can now act like commonly identified threat actors, and with it, fundamentally undermine TTP based attribution.

processed.summary:
- Researchers deployed the **Cybersecurity SuperIntelligence (CSI) framework** to simulate five major **APT groups** using LLM agents against AI-driven defenders.
- The study utilized **Enterprise** and **Military** cyber ranges to evaluate the resilience of **TTP-based attribution** in the era of agentic AI.
- A critical finding was **convergent agent behavior**, where disparate AI profiles independently weaponized defensive tools like **Velociraptor** for C2 channels.
- The research suggests that **generative AI** lowers the barrier for sophisticated cyberattacks, rendering traditional forensic attribution based on behavior unreliable.
key_results: 100% compromise rate in Enterprise ranges with 2 to 12 hosts compromised per experiment.
100% defense or stalemate in Military ranges regardless of the APT profile or defender model used.
Emergent weaponization of the Velociraptor endpoint platform as a C2 channel in 80% of Enterprise experiments.
LLM agents successfully replicated specific operational fingerprints of APT28, APT29, APT41, APT44, and Lazarus Group.
Evidence that TTP-based attribution collapses when high-tier adversary behaviors can be synthetically generated by non-state actors.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: CSI Framework researchers demonstrate that LLM agents can replicate nation-state APT behaviors, fundamentally breaking traditional TTP-based forensic attribution.

title: Think Fast: Estimating No-CoT Task-Completion Time Horizons of Frontier AI Models
summary: Many efforts to ensure frontier AI models are safe rely on monitoring their chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning. If models become able to perform sufficiently complex reasoning internally, without explicit thinking tokens, this would undermine such oversight. We measure how well frontier models reason without CoT across a suite of over 30,000 questions spanning 43 benchmarks in domains including math, coding, puzzles, causality, theory-of-mind, and strategic reasoning. To compare models against humans, we estimate the $50\%$-task-completion time horizon (TH): the human time required for tasks a model completes with $50\%$ success rate. We complement this with a $50\%$ reasoning token horizon: the minimum number of o3-mini reasoning tokens needed for tasks a model solves with $50\%$ success rate. We find that the no-CoT $50\%$ TH of frontier models has been doubling roughly every year over the past six years, with GPT-5.5's TH reaching over 3 minutes and reasoning token horizon exceeding 1,500 tokens. Our median estimates predict that frontier no-CoT THs could exceed 7 minutes by 2028, and 25 minutes by 2030, though these projections carry substantial uncertainty. We recommend frontier developers track this explicitly.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates the **internalized reasoning** capacity of frontier models by measuring performance on 30,000 tasks without using explicit **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** tokens.
- Introduces the **Time Horizon (TH)** metric, which maps model success rates to the human time required to complete the same task, providing a cross-domain evaluation framework.
- Analyzes the trajectory of **latent reasoning** capabilities, finding that no-CoT performance has doubled annually for the past six years, impacting **AI Safety** monitoring strategies.
- Proposes a **Reasoning Token Horizon** to equate model performance to the number of reasoning tokens (e.g., o3-mini) required to solve tasks at a 50 percent success rate.
key_results: Frontier models' no-CoT time horizon has doubled approximately every year for the past six years.
GPT-5.5 class models achieve a no-CoT time horizon of over 3 minutes, matching complex human problem-solving.
The 50 percent reasoning token horizon for frontier models currently exceeds 1,500 tokens.
Projections suggest no-CoT capabilities could reach 25 minutes of human-equivalent reasoning by 2030.
Increasingly complex internal reasoning may render safety monitoring via visible CoT tokens ineffective.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that frontier models' internal reasoning capacity doubles annually, potentially bypassing safety oversight protocols dependent on visible tokens.

title: ANNS-AMP: Accelerating Approximate Nearest Neighbor Search via Adaptive Mixed-Precision Computing
summary: Approximate nearest neighbor search(ANNS) is a critical kernel in modern applications such as LLM and recommendation systems.However,its efficiency is fundamentally limited by the need to compute distances between a query and a massive number of high-dimensional vectors,most of which are non-neighbors.Existing approaches reduce redundancy via index optimization or early termination,but remain constrained by fixed-precision computation,leading to unnecessary arithmetic and memory bandwidth overhead.This paper presents ANNS-AMP,an adaptive mixed-precision framework and accelerator that adapts the precision of distance computation to the characteristics of queries and data distribution.The key insight is that different regions of the vector space require different levels of precision to preserve top-k accuracy.ANNS-AMP leverages the clustered structure of PQ-based indices and introduces a lightweight predictor to determine cluster-level precision at runtime based on features such as scale,radius,and query distance.To efficiently realize variable-precision execution,we design a bit-serial accelerator with a bit-interleaved data layout,enabling throughput to scale with reduced precision while mitigating memory bandwidth bottlenecks and load imbalance through a greedy scheduling strategy.Moreover,the runtime predictor can also reuse the bit-serial computing array for efficient runtime prediction and can be fitted to the ANNS pipeline without performance penalty.According to our experiments on representative datasets,ANNS-AMP achieves 163.76x,10.57x,and 2.06x performance speedups on average,and reduces average energy consumption by 1100.00x,39.41x,and 6.66x compared to CPU,GPU,and customized ANNS accelerator baselines,respectively,while maintaining accuracy loss below 2.7%.These results demonstrate that adaptive mixed-precision computing is a promising direction for efficient large-scale ANNS.

processed.summary:
• ANNS-AMP introduces an **adaptive mixed-precision framework** for vector search, optimizing the distance computation between queries and massive vector datasets.
• The architecture utilizes a **bit-serial accelerator** with a bit-interleaved data layout, allowing throughput to scale dynamically as precision requirements decrease.
• A **lightweight runtime predictor** determines precision levels at the cluster level by analyzing vector distribution features like scale, radius, and query distance.
• A **greedy scheduling strategy** is implemented to mitigate memory bandwidth bottlenecks and load imbalances inherent in variable-precision processing.
key_results: 163.76x performance speedup compared to traditional CPU baselines.
10.57x speedup over GPU-based ANNS implementations.
6.66x energy reduction compared to existing customized ANNS accelerators.
Maintains top-k accuracy with less than a 2.7% loss across representative datasets.
Integrates a runtime predictor that reuses computing arrays without incurring performance penalties.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ANNS-AMP, an adaptive mixed-precision accelerator providing 10.57x GPU speedup for efficient large-scale vector search.

title: From Privacy to Workflow Integrity: Communication-Graph Metadata in Autonomous Agent Interoperability
summary: Agent-interoperability protocols such as A2A and MCP standardize what agents say to one another, but assume address-based transport over HTTP(S). Such transports protect message content, increasingly with end-to-end encryption. What they leave in the clear is the communication graph: which agent contacts which, when, and how often. In agent systems this graph is more consequential than a privacy framing suggests. Endpoints are often capability-labeled, workflows are structured and chained, and interactions are coupled to real actions, so an observer recovers more than past relationships. It can infer the pending workflow, the task being assembled and the action likely to follow. At machine speed, it can act on that inference before the workflow completes. The threat is therefore one of workflow integrity, not privacy alone: predictive leverage over autonomous action. We give a threat model for the agent communication graph; identify what makes agent metadata distinctively revealing (semanticity, prospectivity, actuation); define transport- and bootstrap-layer privacy properties and weigh candidate transports (SimpleX/SMP, Tor, mixnets) against them; and present an A2A case study in which a metadata-protecting binding is expressible but surfaces the protocol's identity assumptions. We test these on a generative model anchored to a real A2A capture. From passive metadata alone, with no payloads, a classifier recovers a task's class well above chance, from only the workflow's opening; applied together, the properties drive that recovery sharply back toward chance. Beyond what an observer can recover, we measure the leverage of acting on the leak: from a workflow's opening and under a fixed budget, an adversary choosing which workflows to act on realizes in this model most of a clairvoyant attacker's advantage over a metadata-blind one, and the same properties suppress it.

processed.summary:
• Evaluates the security risks of **autonomous agent interoperability** protocols (A2A, MCP) regarding the **communication-graph metadata** leaked during transport.
• Proposes a new threat model centered on **workflow integrity**, arguing that metadata is distinctively revealing due to its semanticity, prospectivity, and connection to real-world actuation.
• Benchmarks privacy-preserving transport layers—including **SimpleX/SMP, Tor, and mixnets**—to determine their effectiveness in masking agent interactions.
• Demonstrates how attackers can use generative models to perform **predictive interference**, identifying task types from the opening of a workflow to gain a competitive advantage.
key_results: Classifiers can identify task types well above chance using only passive communication metadata.
Adversaries can predict and act on pending workflows before they complete, compromising execution integrity.
Transport-layer privacy properties significantly reduce an attacker's predictive leverage back toward chance levels.
A2A protocol identity assumptions create friction when implementing strong metadata-protecting bindings.
Metadata leakage allows a budget-constrained attacker to achieve near-clairvoyant performance in selecting workflows to disrupt.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that agent metadata leaks allow adversaries to predict and disrupt workflows before they complete.

title: Decision-Aware Evaluation of Physics-Informed Surrogates
summary:
Physics-informed machine learning is often assessed by curve error, although engineering use depends on downstream decisions: ranking candidates, avoiding infeasible designs and limiting regret. We introduce pinn-gym, an open benchmark for material-conditioned lattice design that couples a transparent reduced-order crush-and-impact oracle with five printable polymer cards, dimensionless force-response targets and a protocol spanning curve fidelity, physical admissibility, top-k retrieval and mass regret.
  Across per-material, pooled and cross-material settings, low nRMSE is frequently insufficient to identify useful design selections. Physics-informed losses alter trade-offs rather than monotonically improving all metrics, and dimensionless conditioning improves comparability without making transfer symmetric. The benchmark is not a certified material model; within the released oracle, candidate generator and material cards, pinn-gym provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML surrogates as decision systems rather than curve predictors alone.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **pinn-gym**, an open-source benchmark specifically designed for evaluating **Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML)** surrogates based on decision-centric performance.
* Moves beyond traditional **curve error (nRMSE)** metrics to focus on downstream engineering outcomes like **physical admissibility**, top-k retrieval, and mass regret.
* Utilizes a **reduced-order oracle** coupled with material-conditioned lattice design to test the ability of surrogates to handle **cross-material transfer** and dimensionless conditioning.
* Highlights that **physics-informed losses** do not always lead to monotonic improvements, instead altering the trade-offs between fidelity and physical consistency.
key_results: Traditional curve-fitting metrics like nRMSE are often insufficient for identifying optimal engineering designs.
Physics-informed losses modify model trade-offs rather than providing uniform improvements across all metrics.
Dimensionless conditioning enhances comparability between materials but fails to make knowledge transfer symmetric.
The benchmark provides a reproducible testbed for evaluating PIML models as active decision systems.
Cross-material and pooled training settings reveal significant variations in surrogate reliability for top-k retrieval tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce pinn-gym to evaluate physics-informed surrogates using decision-centric metrics like mass regret rather than curve error.

title: Explaining Unsupervised Disease Staging in Huntington's Disease: Insights into Model Representations and Clusters
summary: Huntington's disease (HD) is a progressive neurodegenerative disorder that affects motor, cognitive, and behavioral functions, where accurate characterization of disease progression remains essential to improve patient outcome and quality of life. Unsupervised machine learning (ML) approaches have demonstrated the ability to uncover disease progression trajectories and meaningful latent stages from longitudinal data; however, their limited interpretability restricts clinical trust and translation. We extend a previously proposed ML-based disease staging framework by applying an explainability analysis to the extracted feature representations and discovered disease stages. Applied to the Enroll-HD dataset, we first project the learned representations into a lower-dimensional space to intuitively assess whether the resulting clusters align with the progression of established clinical measures. We then use saliency maps to identify the clinical features that most strongly contribute to the learned embeddings over time. Finally, we train a surrogate classifier and apply SHAP to quantify feature importance for cluster assignments and to analyze which clinical variables drive transitions between disease stages. The explainability analysis indicates that the learned embeddings capture clinically meaningful disease structure, aligning with established motor and functional severity scores and exhibiting progressive deterioration across clusters. Within this analysis, SHAP reveals a stratification of disease stages, ranging from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency, consistent with known clinical progression patterns, while also highlighting intra-stage variability.

processed.summary:
• The study extends an **unsupervised machine learning** framework for disease staging by integrating advanced **Explainable AI (XAI)** techniques.
• Researchers utilized **saliency maps** and **SHAP-based surrogate classifiers** to decode how latent representations correlate with clinical motor and cognitive scores.
• The architecture transforms longitudinal data into **learned embeddings**, which are then projected into lower-dimensional spaces to validate clinical cluster alignment.
• This approach addresses the "black box" nature of **longitudinal disease models**, providing a roadmap for clinical trust and translation.
key_results: Learned representations successfully align with established motor and functional severity scores.
Saliency maps effectively isolated specific clinical features influencing temporal embeddings.
SHAP analysis quantified the primary variables driving transitions between disease stages.
The model identifies a clear progression from early cognitive-motor impairment to severe functional dependency.
Explainability tools successfully captured and visualized intra-stage variability within disease clusters.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Enroll-HD researchers utilize SHAP and saliency maps to improve the interpretability of unsupervised disease staging in Huntington's disease.

title: MalSkillBench: A Runtime-Verified Benchmark of Malicious Agent Skills
summary:
AI coding agents such as Claude Code and Gemini CLI increasingly extend themselves with third-party skills: markdown packages bundling natural-language instructions, executable scripts, and tool permissions. Because a skill is at once code and agent-facing instruction, it introduces a supply chain dependency whose risk is neither pure code nor pure prompt. Detection tools have never been measured against verified ground truth spanning this hybrid space, leaving their effectiveness unknown and wild-only evaluations biased.
  We present MalSkillBench, the first runtime-verified benchmark of malicious agent skills: 3,944 malicious skills labeled along a three-dimensional taxonomy of 108 cells. Of these, 3,214 come from a closed-loop Generate-Verify-Feedback pipeline admitting only samples whose malicious behavior fires inside a Docker sandbox under system-call monitoring and an LLM judge; we add 703 in-the-wild and 4,000 matched benign skills. Our measurements are consistent: code injection reaches 94.5% verification yield but prompt injection only 75.8%, the same fragility that later makes it hard to detect; the wild sample is narrow, dominated by one cryptocurrency-theft campaign (86.6% one behavior, 81% from two accounts) with a small but architecturally new tail attacking the agent control plane; the strongest skill-specific detector reaches 98.4% recall on code injection yet collapses on prompt-injection and agent-control attacks, and wild-only scoring swings the ranking by up to 66 recall points; supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses each see only half of a skill, and no combination recovers the code-instruction relationship. Detecting malicious skills therefore requires reasoning jointly over task intent, code, and instructions. We release the dataset, pipeline, baselines, and results.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **MalSkillBench**, the first runtime-verified benchmark specifically designed to evaluate malicious **AI agent skills** across a three-dimensional taxonomy.
* Utilizes a **Generate-Verify-Feedback (GVF)** pipeline that validates malicious behavior within a **Docker sandbox** using system-call monitoring and LLM-based judging.
* Analyzes the unique **supply chain risk** where third-party agent skills combine executable code with natural-language instructions, a hybrid space current scanners fail to address.
* Benchmarks existing defenses, revealing that unified reasoning over **task intent, code, and instructions** is required to secure agent control planes.
key_results: Created a dataset of 3,944 malicious skills and 4,000 benign samples for agent safety evaluation.
Verified code injection attacks achieve a 94.5% yield, whereas prompt injection is significantly more fragile at 75.8%.
Identified that 86.6% of in-the-wild malicious skills originate from a single cryptocurrency-theft campaign.
Found that top-tier detectors achieve 98.4% recall on code injection but fail entirely against agent-control plane attacks.
Demonstrated that combining existing supply-chain scanners and prompt-injection defenses fails to recover the code-instruction relationship.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: MalSkillBench researchers release a verified benchmark exposing how hybrid code-prompt attacks bypass current AI agent security frameworks.

title: Explicit Evidence Grounding via Structured Inline Citation Generation
summary: As AI systems become more widely adopted, the demand for factual and faithful generation grows. Properly attributing information through citations becomes, therefore, crucial. This work introduces FullCite, a framework that, in contrast to most previous works, generates structured inline citations linking each claim to both its source document and supporting evidence. FullCite proposes three strategies to inline citation generation: prompt-based generation, constrained decoding over a citation grammar, and posthoc span alignment. Using three question answering benchmarks, namely, ASQA, BioASQ, and ExpertQA, we assess citation quality and faithfulness along three dimensions: document-level correctness, evidence span identification, and claim-citation faithfulness. Our evaluation shows that while LLMs are generally effective at identifying relevant documents, they struggle to identify the precise supporting spans within them. This gap suggests that achieving faithful attributed QA will require research to place greater emphasis on precise evidence span identification.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **FullCite**, a framework for generating **structured inline citations** that map LLM-generated claims to specific document sources and exact evidence spans.
* Employs three distinct technical strategies: **prompt-based generation**, **constrained decoding** using a citation grammar, and **posthoc span alignment**.
* Benchmarks the system using **ASQA, BioASQ, and ExpertQA** to measure document-level correctness, span identification, and claim faithfulness.
* Reveals a performance gap where LLMs succeed at document retrieval but fail to achieve **precise evidence span identification**, highlighting a key area for future RAG research.
key_results: FullCite provides granular evidence grounding that exceeds standard document-level attribution.
Constrained decoding maintains citation syntax integrity for automated parsing and verification.
The framework identifies a significant deficiency in current LLMs' ability to pinpoint specific text spans.
Evaluation confirms that document retrieval success does not equate to faithful claim grounding.
Posthoc alignment is validated as a fallback for models with weaker instruction-following capabilities.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FullCite to improve RAG faithfulness by linking LLM claims to specific structured evidence spans.

title: A machine-learning-assisted progressive digit-randomness screening framework for detecting non-random patterns in raw numerical research data
summary: Raw numerical datasets remain less systematically examined in integrity screening than images, plagiarism, or summary-statistic inconsistencies. We developed the Fabrication-risk Digit Randomness Screening model (FDRS), a statistical and machine-learning framework for detecting non-random digit-pattern irregularities in numerical research data. FDRS integrates single- and joint-decimal-digit tests, Cramer's V, entropy metrics, Kullback-Leibler divergence, digit-preference indices, progressive subsampling, and semi-supervised risk scoring. It was evaluated using an instrument-derived enzymatic absorbance dataset (RawData, n=253) and a blinded manually simulated irregular dataset (ErrData, n=255). RawData showed no significant deviation in single third-decimal-digit analysis, whereas ErrData showed a significant deviation. In joint third-fourth decimal digit analysis, ErrData showed higher Cramer's V, lower normalized entropy, higher KL divergence, and a more persistent progressive-subsampling deviation signal. In internal validation, Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved the highest AUC (0.98395) and lowest Brier score (0.048439), while Random Forest achieved the highest accuracy (0.926667) and balanced accuracy (0.935). RawData received a low ensemble risk score of 0.124627 and was classified as Grade 0; ErrData received a score of 0.740760 and was classified as Grade 3. External real-world benchmarks supported graded risk stratification: three datasets without identified public post-publication concerns were classified as Grade 0 or 1, whereas two datasets from publicly questioned or institutionally handled articles were classified as Grade 2 or 3. FDRS can prioritize raw numerical datasets for further review by integrating interpretable statistical and machine-learning features. It is an auxiliary digit-structure screening tool, not standalone evidence of fabrication or misconduct.

processed.summary:
- The **FDRS framework** integrates statistical metrics (KL divergence, entropy, Cramer's V) with **machine learning** to identify non-random digit patterns in numerical research data.
- It employs a **progressive subsampling** strategy and semi-supervised risk scoring to detect subtle manual data fabrication that standard integrity checks might miss.
- ML benchmarks show **Elastic-net Logistic Regression** achieving an AUC of 0.984, effectively classifying data into a four-tier **risk stratification** system.
- The system serves as an **AI-driven evaluation tool** for research integrity, prioritizing high-risk datasets for human-in-the-loop review.
key_results: Elastic-net Logistic Regression achieved a high AUC of 0.98395 for fraud detection.
Random Forest models reached 92.67% accuracy and 93.5% balanced accuracy.
Successfully differentiated between authentic enzymatic absorbance data and manually simulated irregular data.
External validation correctly identified datasets from institutionally handled or questioned articles.
Implemented a four-tier grading system (Grade 0 to Grade 3) for automated dataset risk assessment.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FDRS, an ML framework using digit-randomness patterns to detect numerical data fabrication with 0.98 AUC.

title: Learning Explicit Behavioral Models with Adaptive Questions and World-Model Probes
summary: Interactive agents trained only against task return can achieve high scores while failing to represent the mechanisms that make their actions succeed. This makes brittle behavior difficult to diagnose and limits adaptation when environment dynamics change. Existing LLM reflection and policy-code repair can revise behavior from failed trajectories, but questions and world-understanding tests are usually used only after training. We introduce an Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM), a trainable behavioral model that couples task performance with evidence-grounded question answering and executable mechanism prediction. An ESBM represents behavior through typed predicates, weighted rules, bounded options and mechanism memory; the mechanism layer predicts symbolic events, object changes, rewards and terminal consequences under action interventions. After each rollout, adaptive questions and active world-model probes convert score failures, QA errors and transition-prediction errors into constraints for local ESBM edits. Candidate models are selected by a multi-criterion rule that jointly evaluates task score, answerability and active world-model consistency. Under the tested Atari-style protocols, ESBM learns high-scoring policies while producing explicit answers and executable mechanism predictions, indicating that adaptive questions can serve as both training pressure and reusable benchmarks for mechanistic policy learning in this setting.

processed.summary:
- Introduces the **Explicit Symbolic Behavioral Model (ESBM)**, a framework that integrates task performance with **symbolic predicates** and executable mechanism predictions to improve agent transparency.
- Employs a mechanism layer to track **object changes** and terminal consequences, allowing the agent to predict the results of action interventions using a **mechanism memory**.
- Utilizes **adaptive questions** and **active world-model probes** to translate failures into symbolic constraints, enabling local edits to the behavioral model during the training loop.
- Demonstrates that coupling task scores with **evidence-grounded QA** creates a dual-purpose system for both training pressure and reusable mechanistic benchmarks.
key_results: ESBM achieves high-scoring policies comparable to standard agents while maintaining explicit reasoning.
Adaptive questions effectively serve as a training signal for grounding agent behavior in world dynamics.
The model successfully predicts symbolic events and rewards under specific action interventions.
Transition-prediction errors are converted into constraints for more efficient policy refinement.
Multi-criterion evaluation ensures a balance between task score, answerability, and consistency.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce ESBM, a symbolic behavioral model using adaptive probes to align task performance with mechanistic world-understanding.

title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Sparse Covariance Estimation in the Vertical-Split Distributed Model
summary:
We study the minimax estimation error for distributed covariance matrix estimation in the vertical-split (feature-split) setting, where two agents each observe different coordinates of $m$ i.i.d. sub-Gaussian samples and communicate a limited number of bits to a central server. While Rahmani et al. [2025] established nearly tight bounds for dense (unstructured) cross-covariance matrices, we investigate whether imposing elementwise $s$-sparsity on the cross-covariance $C_{21}$ can reduce the required communication and sample complexity. In contrast to the horizontal-split setting, where Braverman et al. [2016] showed that sparsity does not reduce communication cost for mean estimation, we prove that sparsity does help for cross-covariance estimation in the vertical split.
  Specifically, we establish minimax lower bounds showing that the communication budget per agent scales as $B_k = Ω(σ^4 d_k\, s' \log(d_1 d_2/s')/\varepsilon^2)$ and the sample complexity for cross-covariance estimation as $m = Ω(σ^4\, s' \log(d_1 d_2/s')/\varepsilon^2)$, where $s' = s \wedge d_{\min}$. For the $1$-sparse case, this yields an exponential improvement from $d_1 d_2$ to $\log(d_1 d_2)$ compared to the dense rate. Our lower bounds are established via Fano's method with an explicit sparse packing using a Varshamov--Gilbert-type argument for signed partial permutation matrices combined with the Conditional Strong Data Processing Inequality of Rahmani et al. [2025]. We show the bounds are tight with a matching achievable scheme, based on covering-net quantization and entry-wise hard thresholding, that attains the $s$-sparse lower bound up to polylogarithmic factors.

processed.summary:
- Investigates the **minimax estimation error** for distributed covariance estimation in vertical-split architectures where features are partitioned across multiple agents.
- Proves that **elementwise s-sparsity** significantly reduces **communication overhead** and sample complexity, identifying a critical difference from horizontal-split settings.
- Employs **Fano's method** and a sparse packing strategy to establish lower bounds for communication budgets per agent.
- Proposes a matching **achievable scheme** utilizing **covering-net quantization** and hard thresholding to reach near-optimal performance within polylogarithmic factors.
key_results: Established that communication budget per agent scales based on sparsity and feature dimensionality.
Proved that sparsity provides an exponential improvement in communication for 1-sparse cases (from linear to logarithmic).
Developed a minimax lower bound for sample complexity in cross-covariance estimation.
Showed that sparsity helps in vertical-split models even though it often fails to reduce costs in horizontal-split mean estimation.
Validated theoretical bounds with a practical scheme using entry-wise hard thresholding and quantization.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers prove that sparsity exponentially reduces communication costs for cross-covariance estimation in vertical-split distributed learning environments.

title: The Three-Ring Architecture: Governing Agents in the Era of On-Platform Organisations
summary: The current phase of enterprise AI deployment faces a structural failure: organisations are acquiring agentic capability without the infrastructure to govern it. The result is expected to reproduce the error of the first wave of AI deployment: decentralised intelligence without a federation layer leading to a 95% project failure rate. This paper formalises the Three-Ring Architecture as the governing infrastructure of the on-platform organisation. Ring 1 is the existing production architecture; Ring 2 is the M2 federation layer built on strategies-based agentic AI; Ring 3 is the LLM-based frontier intelligence layer. Ring 2 constitutes, in the technically exact sense, the operating system of the agentic enterprise - performing at the organisational level what a computing OS performs at the device level: resource abstraction, process coordination, permission enforcement, and a stable platform for compounding intelligence. A central contribution is the formal distinction between Ring 2 and Ring 3 risk profiles. Strategies-based agents operate within a deterministic framework: their consequences are traceable, their permissions enforceable, their deviations recoverable. LLM-based agents introduce a categorically distinct risk: a non-deterministic actor whose deviations propagate through complex organisational systems without retrospective traceability. Ring 2 is not a useful addition - it is a necessary condition of control and compliance. A further consequence: every improvement in LLM capability is a structural tailwind for this architecture. More capable non-deterministic actors produce larger consequences when they deviate. The governance requirement scales with capability. The architecture has been validated across a decade of deployment in financial services, government, procurement, and compliance among other sectors.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **Three-Ring Architecture** to bridge the governance gap in enterprise AI, aiming to prevent the 95% failure rate associated with decentralized intelligence.
- Defines **Ring 2 (M2 Federation Layer)** as a strategies-based operating system for the enterprise, providing resource abstraction and permission enforcement.
- Categorically separates **deterministic strategies-based agents** from **non-deterministic LLM-based actors** to ensure traceable and recoverable organizational workflows.
- Establishes that the necessity for structured governance scales directly with **frontier intelligence capabilities**, as more capable LLMs create larger non-deterministic risks.
key_results: Formalization of the Three-Ring Architecture for governing on-platform organizations.
Characterization of Ring 2 as the 'Operating System' of agentic enterprises.
Technical distinction between deterministic (Ring 2) and non-deterministic (Ring 3) risk profiles.
Evidence that Ring 2 is a necessary condition for regulatory compliance and control.
Validation of architecture through a decade of deployment in finance, government, and procurement.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose a Three-Ring Architecture to provide a deterministic governance layer for non-deterministic LLM-based enterprise agents.

title: OffQ: Taming Structured Outliers in LLM Quantization by Offsetting
summary: Low-bit quantization has been widely adopted to accelerate the inference of large language models (LLMs) by significantly reducing computational cost and memory usage. However, activation outliers pose a major challenge to effective quantization, often leading to notable performance degradation. In this paper, we introduce OffQ, a method designed to mitigate activation outliers in low-bit quantization through a novel offsetting mechanism. Specifically, OffQ first identifies a low-dimensional outlier subspace in the activations using a proposed top-1 PCA, and then concentrates high-magnitude activations into 1 channel via rotation. OffQ then absorbs this concentrated outlier channel by converting its magnitude into a shared offset, thereby reducing the standard deviation of the activations. This offsetting strategy enables effective W4A4KV4 quantization of LLMs using deployment-friendly uniform-grid and uniform-precision quantization. Extensive experiments across diverse LLM architectures and benchmarks demonstrate that OffQ outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, consistently improving model accuracy while preserving low-bit efficiency.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **OffQ**, a quantization technique that mitigates **activation outliers** in LLMs using a novel **offsetting mechanism**.
- Employs **top-1 PCA** to identify outlier subspaces and rotates activations to concentrate high-magnitude values into a single, manageable channel.
- Converts the concentrated outlier channel into a **shared offset**, effectively reducing the standard deviation of activations and maximizing bit-grid utility.
- Enables high-performance **W4A4KV4 quantization** while remaining compatible with **deployment-friendly** uniform-grid and uniform-precision hardware.
key_results: Successfully implements W4A4KV4 quantization with minimal performance degradation.
Identifies low-dimensional outlier subspaces using a novel top-1 PCA approach.
Reduces activation variance by absorbing concentrated outliers into a shared offset.
Outperforms current state-of-the-art quantization baselines across multiple LLM architectures.
Maintains standard uniform-grid efficiency, avoiding the need for complex custom kernels.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce OffQ to stabilize W4A4KV4 quantization by concentrating and offsetting activation outliers via PCA-based rotation.

title: Beyond Post-hoc Explanation: Toward Glassbox AI via Probabilistic Mediation
summary: Large language models are rapidly becoming infrastructural components in high-stakes institutional settings, including public administration, legal reasoning, and healthcare, where opacity is not merely inconvenient but institutionally and legally untenable. Existing approaches to explainability are predominantly post-hoc, offering unstable, non-contestable accounts that have no formal relationship to the reasoning process that produced the output. We argue that the problem is not the absence of explanation but the absence of structured reasoning in the first place. This paper makes the case for a fundamentally different architecture, which we call the Glassbox Framework, in which Bayesian networks serve as transparent, ante-hoc mediation layers for generative models. Bayesian networks encode domain knowledge, causal assumptions, and probabilistic dependencies before inference occurs, enabling auditable reasoning traces, uncertainty quantification, and contestable outputs. We characterise the architecture of this framework and ground it in a benefit eligibility scenario, identifying the foundational challenges spanning semantic alignment, dynamic model construction, probabilistic grounding, and human governance that must be solved to realise it at scale. By shifting from post-hoc explanation to ante-hoc probabilistic mediation, this work outlines a principled path toward AI systems that are not only powerful but fundamentally accountable.

processed.summary:
• Proposes the **Glassbox Framework**, which replaces unstable post-hoc explanations with **Bayesian networks** acting as transparent, ante-hoc mediation layers.
• Shifts AI design toward **structured reasoning** by encoding domain knowledge and causal dependencies directly into the inference process.
• Enables formal **uncertainty quantification** and auditable reasoning traces, making LLM outputs legally and institutionally contestable.
• Identifies key research hurdles in **semantic alignment** and dynamic model construction to bridge generative models with probabilistic grounding.
key_results: Conceptualization of ante-hoc probabilistic mediation as a superior alternative to post-hoc rationalization.
Definition of an architecture where Bayesian networks serve as the source of truth for reasoning traces.
Introduction of 'contestable outputs' as a metric for AI accountability in high-stakes settings.
Mapping of four foundational challenges: semantic alignment, dynamic construction, probabilistic grounding, and governance.
Validation of the framework through a practical benefit eligibility scenario for institutional use.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose Glassbox AI, using Bayesian mediation to replace post-hoc rationalization with structured, auditable reasoning traces.
